title: Merck & Co. Inc. (MRK) — Intrinsic Value & Prospect Score
description: Opus 4.6 independent discounted cash flow (DCF) intrinsic value analysis of Merck & Co. Inc. (MRK). Economic Prospect Score: 63/100 — Moderate Prospect.

fcfGrowth.title: FCF Growth Rate (Y1-Y10)
fcfGrowth.rationale: Merck & Co. Inc. is projected to grow FCF at 5% annually, reflecting its competitive position, sector dynamics, and historical compounding rate.

discountRate.title: Discount Rate (WACC)
discountRate.rationale: A 9.0% WACC balances Merck & Co. Inc.'s earnings quality and balance sheet strength against sector volatility and macroeconomic risk factors.

terminalGrowth.title: Terminal Growth Rate
terminalGrowth.rationale: A 2.5% terminal rate assumes long-run growth converges toward nominal GDP, appropriate for a mature S&P 500 constituent.

thesisTitle: Merck & Co. Inc.: A Fundamental Assessment
thesisParagraphs: Merck & Co. Inc. (MRK) is a large-cap constituent of the S&P 500 with established market position and consistent cash flow generation. The company's competitive advantages and market share provide a degree of pricing power and earnings stability that supports a premium valuation.

My DCF applies a 5% FCF growth rate reflecting the company's historical compounding ability and forward outlook, with a 9.0% discount rate capturing sector-specific risks and capital market conditions. The valuation is sensitive to assumptions about sustainable growth — investors should stress-test these inputs against their own convictions.
verdictFooter: Opus 4.6 independent analysis. Data via public filings (Mar 20, 2026).

faq.question: What is Merck & Co. Inc.'s intrinsic value according to Opus?
faq.answer: Based on a 10-year DCF with 5% FCF growth and 9.0% WACC, Opus calculates MRK's intrinsic value at approximately $105.35 per share, suggesting the stock is fairly valued at $110.00.

faq.question: What assumptions drive MRK's valuation?
faq.answer: The key drivers are the FCF growth rate (5%) and discount rate (9.0%). A 1% increase in growth adds roughly 10-15% to the intrinsic value. These assumptions reflect Opus's independent assessment of the company's competitive position and risk profile.

faq.question: Should I buy or sell based on this analysis?
faq.answer: This is not financial advice. DCF models are inherently subjective — small changes in assumptions create large swings in output. Use this as one data point among many in your investment research process.

prospectVerdictDetail: Merck's Keytruda is the best-selling drug in history ($25B+ annual sales) but faces a patent cliff in 2028 that looms large. The company is aggressively building a pipeline through acquisitions (Prometheus, Acceleron) but needs multiple blockbusters to fill the gap. Gardasil growth is strong globally but facing unexpected pressure in China.
prospectKeyRisks: Keytruda patent cliff in 2028 creating a $25B+ revenue hole that no single drug can replace
Gardasil demand weakness in China raising questions about international growth assumptions
Pipeline execution risk — Merck needs 3-5 blockbuster drugs to replace Keytruda, and drug development has a 90% failure rate
prospectKeyCatalysts: Subcutaneous Keytruda formulation extending the franchise beyond patent expiry and differentiating from biosimilars
Pneumococcal vaccine (V116) becoming a multi-billion dollar franchise to partially offset Keytruda losses
ADC pipeline (from Daiichi Sankyo partnership) delivering next-generation oncology drugs that leverage Keytruda's physician relationships
prospectMethodology: Score is based on three pillars: Competitive Momentum (0-35), Moat Durability (0-35), and Sentiment & Catalysts (0-30), totaling 0-100. Each pillar is broken into individually scored factors with transparent rationale. Data sources include FY2025 10-K filings, analyst consensus estimates, news sentiment analysis, and competitive landscape assessment. The score is forward-looking and represents economic prospect over a 2-3 year horizon.
